author: jochembrouwer
content:
Let me point out that these tests are crafted for a block scope (not multi-block scope). The transactions do not read the same slots, they follow a pointer (cursor). The loop does P=SLOAD(POINTER_SLOT), and then SLOAD(P), SLOAD(P+1), until there is not enough gas left (a predetermined threshold value). If this drops below, it will SSTORE(POINTER_SLOT, whatever_slot_we_were_about_to_read)
It thus reads different slots across all transactions.
Note that optimistic parallelization will cause tx2 to read the same slots as tx1 (you thus have to sequentially execute those to read all slots, the optimistic/greedy optimizaton to prefetch slots does not work here (but will work with BALs as you get the value of the POINTER_SLOT for tx2..txN in the BAL)

We could (and should) however also target different patterns, this one only does linearly increasing, we could also start accessing "random" slots (via keccak) or decreasing (we have this)

For interest: https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/pull/2033/
We plan to merge this, this week

author: jochembrouwer
content: If you have other ideas how to stress test this or other questions please drop them, we want to test all situations 😄

author: jochembrouwer
content:
We target also the worst-case, so this means if necessary we force clients to disk.
In the average case (mainnet case) where we execute blocks where many storage targets (accounts+storage) are frequently used, we will see some benefit.
However, for the worst-case (where an attacker starts querying accounts/storage created/accessed a long time ago) the BAL should have the benefit that we can prefetch the storage (or value is already known due to write in a previous tx). Which is exactly what we want to bench (the max gain % in that case) 😄
